speaker: interviewer
text: Freda Isaksen So you decided to go on vacation with the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went to the farm with the three children. We had such a great time together. Of course I became very closely attached to them. So when I heard the three children had been taken to Auschwitz, it was a-- I mean, everything is traumatic.

speaker: interviewer
text: How can you say one thing is more traumatic than another? You can't. To have two brothers-in-law taken out and shot.

speaker: interviewee
text: But these three children couldn't-- it was a shocking thing to me. And it still is. I have to say, there isn't a day of my life that I don't think of all of this. And then there was the-- the other child. Micael's sister lived in Oslo. And she had stayed in Norway. She had never left. She had stayed in Oslo through the war. So she hadn't gone to Sweden and then returned. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was 32-years-old when the Germans invaded. She had suffered all her life with headaches. And nobody really knew what it was. But when the war-- the Germans invaded, she became much worse. She was hospitalized and they found that she had a brain tumor, which had probably had been fairly dormant. But because of all the trauma of the invasion, it had become a great deal worse. And she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So her husband was left with little Eleanor, three years-old. Well, they went to live with his mother so the mother could take care of the little girl. Remember, the Germans were already in Norway.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day a policeman came to [? Jak. ?] The father's name was [? Jak. ?] And he said-- he said, I know-- a-- a Norwegian policeman, not Jewish. He said, I know that your family is going to be taken away. He said, I know this. He said, please, let me take little Eleanor. He said, I will bring her up with my-- with my own family. I will raise her as my own child. The Germans will not know who she is. She'll just blend in with our family. And if you return, we'll-- she'll be your child. He said, please let me take her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the father was willing to do this. But the grandmother said, they're-- I'm an old lady and a three-year-old girl. What are they going to do to me? The Germans aren't going to do anything to me. Know you, why-- why would they? An old lady? She wouldn't let the child go. So they were eventually taken, all three of them. Little Eleanor was lost too. So what shall I say? That's the story. Did you have regular contact with your family throughout all this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were war years. You see, when I was in Norway, I was not allowed to send mail through you know, sort of the thing, they couldn't get mail from Norway to England. I did get some mail through-- you know, the cruise ships used to come into Trondheim. And you know, Trondheim's not a big city. You don't have much to do. So before the war, people used to go down and see the cruise ships coming in, and maybe speak to the people. There was a man we spoke to who was-- oh, his name was [? Ruby. ?] He was a fairly well-known musician. We found that out. We didn't know it at the time. It seems he was a very-- we found out later he was a very-- I forget his second name. [? Ruby ?] somebody. He was a very well-known musician. And he-- when he left, we spoke to him on the streets of-- of-- of Trondheim, and he told us he was a musician. He said, if you ever want to come to America-- this was before the war-- you know, here's my address. You look me up, he said. Oh, he told me his name. I don't think he gave us his address. He said, you'll be able to find me in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: We thought, well, what kind of a [? bragger ?] is this? We'll be able to find this Ruby, whatever his name was, in New York. OK, forgot all about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: When all this happened, we thought, I wanted to get in touch with my parents. I wrote a letter to Ruby, whatever his name was. He told me-- see, it's 50 years ago. I had some way of contacting him, and I didn't have an address.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he told me who he-- he had told us who he worked for, some kind of a music company. I wrote the letter to this music company with a letter to my parents. And I said, is it possible for you to get hold of this Ruby so-and-so? If you can, please give him this letter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I found out later that he had sent-- this man received the letter and sent it on to my family in London. So that was the way I had to communicate. It wasn't a big communication, but at least my parents knew where I was. That's the only way I had of-- had of communicating with them. And it's a little vague in my mind, because I say, it's all these years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know that you were leaving to go to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew about it through my cousin in New York. You  see, because this was before the United States came into the war. So Joshua Trachtenberg, who was also pretty well-- he was a pretty well-known rabbi. He informed them, that what he was doing, and that he had helped us come to the United States. Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did the entire journey from Stockholm to San Francisco take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during that time that you were in Sweden and traveling around the world, basically, did you continue to observe religious holidays in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. When we were in-- well, we were in Holland. That was the border between Norway and Sweden, and it was Pesach. And we were going to make a seder, because we'd always had a bountiful seder.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got some-- we wrote-- some people in Stockholm sent us some matzahs and salami. We didn't have Haggadahs or anything. We tinted water red and that was wine. And we made a seder with paper napkins as plates. And we had a seder. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That was very important to us. Because, well, we-- we left our country because we were Jews. And we weren't going to forsake our religion. Yeah. And I-- I was Orthodox. I kept an Orthodox household. I can't say we kept strictly Orthodox when we lived in Sweden, because it was almost impossible. But we observed the holidays. And when I came to the United States, I was a practiced Orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [LAUGHS] No. The reason I changed was because when my children became old enough to go to religious school, well, we'd kind of gave-- gave up on [? the-- ?] [? reason-- ?] [? the reason ?] I gave up on the Orthodox-- Orthodox is this. I kept a kosher household the first-- when we were first here. And I used to walk a long way to get kosher meat. I didn't have a car. I used to push these kids along and I'd-- and take them to buy kosher meat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day I walked in the-- in the-- it had a kosher sign on the window. I came in there and there was bacon in the case there. I said, what's going on here? I said, this is-- what, you have bacon here? Isn't this kosher? He said, it's kosher style, he said. Oh, my god. Kosher style. So I thought, my god, here have I been walking all this long way to get kosher meat and I've been eating treyf anyway, so what the heck? I'll go to the supermarket and buy kosher meat, or treyf meat. So from then on, no more kosher. OK. But we always observed the holidays, always have to this day. So came the time when my children had to be-- go to religious school. We thought, well, we won't send them to the Orthodox, we'll send them to the conservative. They wouldn't go, the two boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't like it. We don't like it. We don't understand. There's so much Hebrew. They don't-- we don't like it. We don't want to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after I had this for a few weeks I thought, well, what are we going to do? I can't force them to do something they're going to hate. Well, we're not going to give up. But let's try the Reformed. Let's try it. We switched from Orthodox to Conservative, maybe we'll switch from Conservative to Reformed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sent them to the Reformed to-- well, we started with University Temple. That didn't work out. We didn't seem to fit in there. So we sent them to Temple Isaiah, and, oh, well, they-- I mean, they-- they loved it. They went right through bar mitzvah, confirmation, married by-- by-- by Rabbi Lewis. I mean, they went right through. They-- no-- no question about that. So we were-- became Reformed. OK, we still, still-- I still belong to Temple Isaiah, and very active.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of an occupation did your husband carve out for himself here in the United States, after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he had always been-- he'd always been in gentlemen's work, never women's. But when he came here, he realized that he was just going to be a tailor here. There was-- he was not going to earn much of a living. And it would be much better for him to go into women's works. See, my husband was very highly skilled. So he went into women's. And he-- he worked with such people as Don Loper, Rudi Gernreich. He did very custom work, very highly skilled work. And he worked mainly as a sample maker. He made clothes for Elizabeth Taylor and the film stars. And he didn't want to do that, because it was-- that was individual. He didn't want to do individual work. But he did. And he never liked his work. He never liked the people he worked with. They were, uh, hard people to work with. They were socialists, hard people to work with. He always said, I'm working till the day I'm 62, because I've got to earn a living. And the day I'm 62, I'm quitting. But we were-- we were very clever at saving money. We knew we had to get along. We had no one to turn to. I mean, we both came from families where if you needed a little money, you'd go to your brother. And there was always somebody to help you out, there was no question about it. OK, we both came from families like that. In fact, when we considered coming to the United States, my brother said, you have-- or even after the war, my husband said, you have to come to England. My husband-- see, my brother had my father's business, and he said, he comes here, he says, he's got a ready-made job. He said, he'll come into the business. And my husband wouldn't do that. He said, I'm never going into your family's business. He said, I have to do it on my own. Anyway, so we decided to go. We'd-- we'd saved our money. And we went into real estate. We never had vacations. We never have had any extra luxuries. Even though we raised the three boys, we scrimped and we saved. And we bought real estate. And that's how we made it. And the day he was 62, he retired. And we started to travel all over the world. And that's how we got along. Never had any help from anybody. Just hard work, I'd say. Mostly, I guess, he did all the hard work. Well, I helped, I-- but it was his-- his hard work that did it. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was your third son born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My third son was born after the war, 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, is there anything else that you'd like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. What shall I say? There's so-- you know, so many things happened during the war that it's so painful for me to think-- to think of that, all those-- the wonderful family that I was so attached to. But we never lost touch with them. After the war, those who survived came to visit us. And I've been back to Norway several times, because my own-- my own family don't understand my attitude. I lived 21 years in England. That was my home. I was an English girl. I went to live in Norway. And I only lived there, altogether, four years. And yet, my-- my heart is in Norway. I never get over thinking that Norway is my home. When I go over there and I visit, and I'm welcomed with open arms, and everybody takes me in as if I was born into their family. Of course, I-- I married into the family. Of course, there aren't that many. When I'm talking about everybody, there are nieces and the nephews. There are mostly nieces and nephews left now. And I'm still their aunt, and greet me with open arms. I'm going back again this summer. Mhm. I speak the language, always have. Never forgotten the language.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any message you'd like to leave for the people that are going to be viewing this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the thing I'd like to say is, never forget. In my case, never forgive. Never forget that the Germans-- and I don't say Nazis. Now, a lot of-- I've been reprimanded on this. People say, why do you say Germans? I still say Germans. Maybe I'm right, maybe I'm wrong. Never forget what they did. And never, never let it happen again. We have to fight for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if I've said anything here today that can make you realize that we have to keep up our Jewish faith. Because if we don't, why did all those Jewish people die? What was it all for? If-- if we don't keep up our beliefs, it will have all been in vain. So that's my belief. OK, thank you very much, Freda.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is a picture of my mother and father on their wedding day in London, around 1910.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, their names, Levy and Yetta. Levy Trachtenberg. My mother was Yetta [? Hartstein. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of brothers-- my brother and sisters, Jack, [? Molly, ?] Rose, and Phyllis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Also taken in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my, it was around 1970. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, between '60 and '70. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of Micael, my husband, Micael, and myself on our wedding day in-- September the 13th, 1936, taken in London. And the very next day, I left for Norway.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you repeat your name for the camera?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my name was Freda Trachtenberg. I changed it that day to Freda Isaksen, and at the same time, became a Norwegian citizen. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of Micael, my husband, and the three sons, Leonard, Leif, and John. This was taken on Leonard's wedding day, in Los Angeles. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: 20-- about 20-- about 30 years ago. So it was 19-- it was 1960-- about 1966? Yes, 1960's about-- 19-- 1965 or six.
